title: Prospective association of screen time with binge-eating disorder among adolescents in the United States: The mediating role of depression.
journal: The International journal of eating disorders

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (2016–2021); N=9465 children (aged 9–11 years at baseline)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures 'average daily screen time' as composite variable with no smartphone or social media breakdown. No PDF available to confirm smartphone-specific or SM-specific coefficient extraction. Per FT-IC2 rule: 'generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown' = NOT_MET. Exclusion via FT-EC2.

FT_IC3.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET renders FT-IC3 evaluation moot; paper fails at exposure criterion and is excluded.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (International Journal of Eating Disorders, 2024); not identified as preprint or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable; paper fails at FT-IC2. Screen time variable is composite without smartphone/SM specificity.
notes: EXCLUDED: FT-IC2 NOT_MET. Composite screen time without smartphone or social media specificity.

title: Adolescent smartphone use, sleep, and physical activity: daily associations between sensor-based measures in the adolescent brain cognitive development (ABCD) study.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data; N=791 adolescents (Mage=14.12, 53% female) with EARS passive sensing data

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone application use measured via EARS (Effortless Assessment Research System) passive sensing; reported by time window: daytime/evening (6AM–7:59PM), late-evening (8PM–9:59PM), late-night (10PM–5:59AM). Specific smartphone exposure variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects models testing daily associations between smartphone app use and multiple non-SM outcomes: sleep (sleep onset delay, sleep duration), physical activity (exercise, steps, sedentary time). Standardized beta coefficients reported (P<.001 throughout).

FT_IC4.evidence: Standardized beta coefficients reported for daytime/evening (β=−0.21 to −0.07, P<.001), late-evening (β=−0.05 to −0.02, P<.001), late-night (β=−0.16 to −0.27, P<.001), and within-person effects (β=−0.22 to 0.07, P<.001/P≤.002).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective sensor-based daily-level study using mixed-effects models examining associations between smartphone use and sleep/physical activity outcomes over 3 weeks.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Psychiatry Research, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone use is independent variable; sleep and physical activity are dependent variables. Daily-level cross-sectional associations. FT-EC7 criterion (a) requires smartphone/SM as DV in all analyses—criterion not met. Smartphone is IV throughout.
notes: INCLUDE. Passive sensing of smartphone app use predicting sleep and physical activity outcomes.

title: Social epidemiology of bedtime screen use behaviors and sleep outcomes in early adolescence.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Year 3 (2019–2021), N=10,305 early adolescents (12–13 years)

FT_IC2.evidence: Bedtime screen use measured via 11-item self-report questionnaire assessing media use while in bed: phone/texting (turn phone off, put on silent, leave ringer on, put outside room), watching videos/TV, playing video games, playing music, talking on phone or texting, spending time on social media, browsing Internet, using computer/laptop for studying. Composite mean score calculated across modalities. Specific smartphone and social media measures included.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression analyses and modified Poisson regression examining associations between bedtime screen use (independent variable) and sleep outcomes (caregiver-reported sleep disturbance via SDSC, self-reported sleep duration via MCTQ). Logistic regression for binary sleep disturbance outcome; linear regression for continuous sleep duration outcome. Statistics reported across sociodemographic strata (Tables 4–7).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients and p-values reported in Tables 4–7. Example: bedtime screen use mean score OR=1.36 (95% CI [1.23, 1.50], p<.001) for sleep disturbance; B=−0.43 (95% CI [−0.52, −0.34], p<.001) for sleep duration. Multiple stratified analyses with separate coefficients by sex, household income, and parental education.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Year 3 data using multivariable regression models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Sleep Health, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Bedtime screen use is independent variable predicting sleep outcomes (dependent variables) throughout all reported analyses. Screen use → sleep, not demographic → screen use. FT-EC7 requires smartphone/SM as DV in all analyses; criterion not met. Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria (a), (b), or (c).
notes: INCLUDE. Bedtime screen use (including phone/SM-specific items) is IV predicting sleep disturbance and sleep duration. Cross-sectional analyses. Smartphone/SM as independent variable, not dependent variable.
